blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (١)
Those who are [themselves] faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah—He has rendered their works fruitless. (1)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَا نُزِّلَ عَلَىٰ مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۙ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بَالَهُمْ (٢)
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—He shall absolve them of their misdeeds and set right their affairs. (2)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ (٣)
That is because the faithless follow falsehood, and the faithful follow the truth from their Lord. That is how Allah draws comparisons for mankind. (3)
فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَضَرْبَ ٱلرِّقَابِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا۟ ٱلْوَثَاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّۢا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَآءً حَتَّىٰ تَضَعَ ٱلْحَرْبُ أَوْزَارَهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَٱنتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَا۟ بَعْضَكُم بِبَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَن يُضِلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٤)
When you meet the faithless in battle, strike their necks. When you have thoroughly decimated them, bind the captives firmly. Thereafter either oblige them [by setting them free] or take ransom, until the war lays down its burdens. That [is Allah’s ordinance]. Had Allah wished He could have taken vengeance on them, but that He may test some of you by means of others. As for those who were slain in the way of Allah, He will not let their works go fruitless. (4)
سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بَالَهُمْ (٥)
He will guide them and set right their affairs, (5)
وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ (٦)
and admit them into paradise, with which He has acquainted them. (6)

thoughtful Standard cleaning and formatting applied. No commentary added. Consistent terminology used.

(Commentary)

The Surah describes the disbelievers (الذين كفروا) with the vile attributes and evil deeds that characterize them, and describes the believers (الذين آمنوا) with their goodly traits and righteous deeds. It then mentions the consequences that follow the attributes of the latter—in terms of blessing and honor—and the attributes of the former—in terms of vengeance and humiliation. In general, the Surah presents a comparison between the two groups regarding their attributes and actions in this world and what results from them in the Hereafter. It also contains some rulings related to fighting (القتال).

It is a Medinan Surah, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "As for those who are faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah, He has let their deeds go astray." (47:1). Turning away (الصد) has been interpreted as averting oneself from the way of Allah, which is Islam, as reported from some commentators. Others have interpreted it as prevention (المنع), meaning their preventing people from believing in the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — to which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was calling them.

The second interpretation is more in accord with the context of the subsequent verses, especially those commanding the believers to kill and capture them.

Thus, what is meant by "those who are faithless" (الذين كفروا) are the disbelievers of Mecca and those who followed them in their disbelief. They used to prevent people from believing in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and would persecute them; they also barred them from the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام).

His saying: أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "He has let their deeds go astray" means He made their deeds wander, such that they do not reach the objectives they intended, which were, in short, to nullify the truth and revive falsehood. Thus, the sentence carries the same meaning as His frequently repeated saying: وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ — "and Allah does not guide the faithless lot." (2:264). Indeed, the Glorified has promised to establish the truth and nullify falsehood, as in His saying: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse." (8:8).

Therefore, the meaning of the "straying" (ضلال) of their deeds is their futility and corruption without reaching their goal. Describing this as "straying" is a form of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَآمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ — "But those who have faith and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—" (47:2) etc. The apparent generality of the beginning of the verse suggests that "those who have faith" refers to anyone who believes and performs righteous deeds (الصالحات). Thus, His saying: وَآمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى مُحَمَّدٍ — "and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad" would be a restrictive qualification (تقييد احترازي) rather than mere emphasis, highlighting that which is the primary object of concern in faith.

His saying: وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ — "and it is the truth from their Lord" is a parenthetical clause (جملة معترضة), and the pronoun refers back to "what has been sent down" (ما نزل).

His saying: كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بالَهُمْ — "He will absolve them of their misdeeds and set right their state." (47:2). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The state (البال) refers to the condition (الحال) and affair (الشأن). Al-Bal also means the heart (القلب); it is said: 'Such a thing occurred to my bal (mind).' The word bal is not used in the plural because it is more ambiguous than its counterparts like hal and shan." End quote.

The "letting deeds go astray" in the previous verse has been contrasted with the "absolving of misdeeds" and "setting right the state" in this verse. This means guiding their faith and righteous deeds to the ultimate goal of felicity (السعادة). This is only completed by the absolution of misdeeds that prevent one from reaching felicity; for this reason, the absolution of misdeeds was joined with the setting right of their state.

The meaning is: Allah has drawn a veil over their misdeeds through pardon and forgiveness, and has set right their state in this world and the Hereafter. As for this world, it is because the true religion is that which accords with the requirements of the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. The primordial nature only requires that which contains its felicity and perfection. Thus, in believing in what Allah has sent down of the religion of nature and acting upon it, there is the rectification of the believers' state in their worldly society. As for the Hereafter, it is because it is the consequence of the worldly life; since its beginning was felicitous, its end shall be likewise. The Exalted said: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى — "and the outcome will be in favor of Godwariness." (20:132).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْباطِلَ وَأَنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْحَقَّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ — "That is because the faithless follow falsehood, and the faithful follow the truth from their Lord." (47:3) etc. This is an explanation of the cause (تعليل) for what was mentioned in the two preceding verses regarding the straying of the disbelievers' deeds and the rectification of the believers' state along with the absolution of their misdeeds.

In qualifying the truth with the phrase مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ — "from their Lord", there is an indication that what is attributed to the Exalted is the truth, while falsehood has no attribution to Him. Therefore, the Glorified took charge of setting right the state of the believers because the path of truth they followed is attributed to Him. As for the disbelievers and their deeds, the Exalted has no concern for them. As for the attribution of their straying to Him in His saying: "He has let their deeds go astray," the meaning of letting their deeds go astray is His not guiding them to righteous and felicitous ends.

The verse contains an indication that the sole criterion for human felicity or wretchedness is the following of truth or the following of falsehood. The reason for this is the attribution of truth to the Exalted, to the exclusion of falsehood.

His saying: كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثالَهُمْ — "Thus does Allah draw for mankind their parables." (47:3) means He clarifies their descriptions to them as they truly are. The use of the demonstrative pronoun for the distant (ذلك) serves to magnify the matter of the parable He has struck.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ — "So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks" (47:4) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded in the three verses regarding the description of the two groups. It is as if it were said: Since the believers are the people of truth and Allah bestows upon them what He bestows, and the disbelievers are the people of falsehood and Allah lets their deeds go astray, then the believers, when they meet the disbelievers, must kill and capture them so that the truth upon which the believers stand may live, and the earth may be purified from the falsehood upon which the disbelievers stand.

His saying: فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ — "So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks": "Meeting" (اللقاء) here refers to meeting in combat. "Striking the necks" (ضرب الرقاب) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its governing verb. The implied meaning is: "Strike the necks—meaning their necks—with a striking." Striking the neck is a metaphor for killing with the sword, because the easiest and swiftest way to kill is by striking the neck with it.

His saying: حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ — "until when you have subdued them, then bind them fast." (47:4). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "Subduing (الإثخان) is to kill extensively and to overcome and conquer the enemy. From it is said: 'Illness subdued him' when it became severe, and 'wounds subdued him'." End quote. In al-Mufradat: "I trusted (وثقت) in him... I relied upon him. I bound him (أوثقته) means I tied him firmly. The word al-wathaq (الوثاق)—with a fatha or kasra on the waw—are two nouns for that with which a thing is bound." End quote. "Until" (حتى) marks the limit (غاية) for striking the necks. The meaning is: Kill them until you have killed extensively among them, then take them as captives by binding them fast and securely. Thus, "binding them fast" refers to taking captives (الأسر). The verse, in making the taking of captives contingent upon subduing the enemy, carries the same meaning as His saying: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly subdued [the enemy] in the land." (8:67).

His saying: فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِداءً — "then either [set them free] with a favor afterwards or [let them go] for a ransom" (47:4) means: capture them, and it follows from this that you either show them favor after the capture by releasing them or enslaving them, or you ransom them for wealth or in exchange for the captives they hold of yours.

His saying: حَتَّى تَضَعَ الْحَرْبُ أَوْزارَها — "until the war lays down its burdens." (47:4). The "burdens" (أوزار) of war are its heavy loads, namely the weapons carried by the combatants. What is meant is the combatants and people of war laying down their weapons, which is a metaphor for the end of the fighting.

From the preceding meaning, the error in the claim of some that this verse abrogates (ناسخة) His saying: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly subdued [the enemy] in the land" (8:67) becomes clear. Their argument was that this Surah was revealed later than Surah al-Anfal and thus abrogates it.

This is incorrect because there is no contradiction between the two verses. The verse in al-Anfal prohibits taking captives before subduing the enemy, while the verse under discussion commands taking captives after subduing them.

Similarly, the claim that His saying: فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ — "then bind them fast" etc., is abrogated by the "Verse of the Sword": فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ — "then slay the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5). This seems to be based on the view that a general rule (العام) revealed after a specific one (الخاص) abrogates it rather than being specified by it. The truth is contrary to this, and the full discussion belongs to the Principles of Jurisprudence (أصول الفقه). The verse also contains jurisprudential discussions whose place is the science of Jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying: ذلِكَ — "That [is the ordinance]" (47:4) means the matter is thus; that is, the judgment of Allah is what has been mentioned in the verse.

His saying: وَلَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَانْتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ — "and had Allah wished He would have taken vengeance on them" (47:4). The pronoun refers to the disbelievers. That is, had Allah willed to take vengeance upon them, He would have done so by destroying them and punishing them without commanding you to fight them.

His saying: وَلكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ — "but [He desires] to test some of you by means of others." (47:4) is an emendation (استدراك) regarding the will to take vengeance. That is, He did not take vengeance upon them directly, but rather commanded you to fight them to test some of you by means of others. Thus, He tests the believers by means of the disbelievers—commanding them to fight them so that the obedient may be distinguished from the disobedient—and He tests the disbelievers by means of the believers, so that the people of wretchedness among them may be distinguished from those who are granted the success to repent from falsehood and return to the truth.

It has thus become clear that His saying: لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ — "to test some of you by means of others" is the explanation of the cause for the rulings mentioned in the verse. The address in "some of you" (بعضكم) is to the collective of believers and disbelievers, though the speech is directed toward the believers.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَلَنْ يُضِلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "As for those who are slain in the way of Allah, He will never let their deeds go astray." (47:4). The speech is phrased as a conditional, and the ruling is general. That is, whoever is killed in the way of Allah—which is struggle (الجهاد) and fighting against the enemies of the religion—He will never nullify the righteous deeds they performed in the way of Allah.

It has been said that what is meant by "those who are slain in the way of Allah" are the martyrs of the Day of Uhud. However, this is a specification without a specifier, while the context is one of generality.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ — "He will guide them and set right their state," (47:5). The pronoun refers to those who were killed in the way of Allah. Thus, the verse and the one following it are to clarify their condition after martyrdom. That is, Allah will guide them to the stations of felicity and honor, and set right their state through forgiveness and pardoning their misdeeds, so they become fit to enter Paradise.

When this verse is joined with His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living with their Lord," (3:169), it becomes clear that the meaning of "setting right their state" is reviving them with a life through which they are fit to be present with their Lord by the lifting of the veil.

It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The reason for the repetition of His saying: 'their state' (بالهم) is that the first instance meant He set right their state in religion and the world, while the second means He will set right their state in the bliss of the Hereafter. Thus, the first is the cause of bliss, and the second is the bliss itself." End quote. The difference between the meaning he mentioned and what we have presented is that His saying: وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ — "and set right their state", according to what we mentioned, is like an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسيري) to His saying: سَيَهْدِيهِمْ — "He will guide them", unlike what he mentioned. Furthermore, the subsequent saying: وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ — "and admit them into paradise" is, according to what he mentioned, like an explanatory apposition to His saying: وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ, unlike what we mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَها لَهُمْ — "and admit them into paradise, which He has made known to them." (47:6). This is the ultimate goal of His guiding them. His saying: عَرَّفَها لَهُمْ — "which He has made known to them" is a circumstantial clause (حال) regarding His admitting them into Paradise. That is, He will admit them into Paradise while He has already made it known to them, either through worldly clarification via revelation and prophethood, or through glad tidings at the time of death, or in the grave, or on the Day of Resurrection, or in all of these stages. This is the meaning suggested by the context.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Ali who said: "A verse in Surah Muhammad is about us, and a verse is about the Umayyads."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated in his Tafsir from his father, from some of our companions, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), a similar report.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying: فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ — "So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks" etc., it is narrated from the Imams of Guidance (peace be upon them): "Captives are of two types: a type taken before the end of the fighting while the war is still ongoing—in this case, the Imam has the choice between killing them or cutting off their hands and feet on opposite sides and leaving them until they bleed out; showing favor (المن) or ransom (الفداء) is not permissible.

The other type are those taken after the war has laid down its burdens and the fighting has ended—in this case, the Imam has the choice regarding them between showing favor, ransom—either with wealth or exchange of persons—enslavement, or striking the necks. If they embrace Islam in either case, all of that is dropped, and their ruling becomes that of the Muslims."

I say: A report with this meaning is narrated in al-Kafi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَلَنْ يُضِلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "As for those who are slain in the way of Allah, He will never let their deeds go astray" (47:4). He said: "It was revealed concerning those of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم who were killed on the Day of Uhud."

I say: You have already known that the verse is general. The use of the future tense (سياق الاستقبال) in His saying: سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ — "He will guide them and set right their state" etc., only accords with generality and the fact that the speech is intended to establish a general principle.

It has been narrated that His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ — "until when you have subdued them, then bind them fast" (47:4) abrogates His saying: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى — "A prophet may not take captives..." (8:67). Also, that His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ — "then slay the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5) abrogates His saying: فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِداءً — "then bind them fast, then either [set them free] with a favor afterwards or [let them go] for a ransom." (47:4). You have already seen in what preceded that the claim of abrogation is not tenable.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ (٧)
O you who have faith! If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady. (7)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَعْسًا لَّهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٨)
As for the faithless, their lot will be to fall [into ruin], and He will render their works fruitless. (8)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٩)
That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down, so He made their works fail. (9)
۞ أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ دَمَّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْثَـٰلُهَا (١٠)
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? Allah destroyed them, and a similar [fate] awaits these faithless. (10)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَأَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (١١)
That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector. (11)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ وَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ (١٢)
Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. As for the faithless, they enjoy and eat like the cattle do, and the Fire will be their [final] abode. (12)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ هِىَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِّن قَرْيَتِكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَا نَاصِرَ لَهُمْ (١٣)
How many a town We have destroyed which was more powerful than your town which expelled you, and they had no helper. (13)
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ كَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم (١٤)
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their conduct is made to seem decorous and who follow their desires? (14)
مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ فِيهَآ أَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن مَّآءٍ غَيْرِ ءَاسِنٍ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن لَّبَنٍ لَّمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُۥ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ خَمْرٍ لَّذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ عَسَلٍ مُّصَفًّى ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ كَمَنْ هُوَ خَـٰلِدٌ فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَسُقُوا۟ مَآءً حَمِيمًا فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعَآءَهُمْ (١٥)
A description of the paradise promised to the Godwary: therein are streams of unstaling water and streams of milk unchanging in flavour, and streams of wine delicious to the drinkers, and streams of purified honey; there will be every kind of fruit in it for them, and forgiveness from their Lord. [Are such ones] like those who abide in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water which cuts up their bowels? (15)

(Commentary)

The verses continue in the same context as the preceding ones.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَنْصُرُوا اللهَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدامَكُمْ "O you who have faith! If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady" (47:7). This is an incitement for them to engage in struggle (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah — and a promise of victory for them if they help Allah, the Exalted. What is meant by their helping Allah is that they struggle in the way of Allah (سبيل الله) such that they fight for the sake of Allah, to support His religion and to exalt the word of truth, not to seek superiority on earth, to acquire spoils of war, or to display gallantry and courage.

What is meant by Allah’s helping them is His facilitating the causes that necessitate their emergence and victory over their enemy, such as casting terror into the hearts of the disbelievers, turning the tide of fortune in favor of the believers against them, and strengthening the hearts of the believers and emboldening them. Accordingly, the coupling of "making the feet steady" with "help" is an instance of coupling the specific with the general. The specific mention of making the feet steady — which is an allusion to emboldening and strengthening the hearts — is due to it being among the most manifest forms of help.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ "As for the faithless, may they be wretched, and He has let their deeds go astray" (47:8). He mentions what is done to the disbelievers immediately after mentioning what is done for the believers who help Allah, in order to contrast their respective conditions.

Wretchedness (التعس) is for a person to fall on his face and remain upon it; its opposite is recovery (الانتعاش), which is to rise after falling on one's face. Thus, His saying: فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ "may they be wretched" means: may they fall into wretchedness. This is a form of invocation against them, similar to His saying: قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ "May Allah assail them! Where do they stray?" (9:30), and قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ "Perish man! How ungrateful is he!" (80:17). It is also possible that it is an informative statement regarding their wretchedness and the futility of the effects of their efforts by way of allusion, for a human being is at his most helpless when he has fallen upon his face.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمالَهُمْ "That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down, so He has let their deeds go to waste" (47:9). What is meant by "what Allah has sent down" is the Quran, the laws, and the rulings that Allah, the Exalted, sent down to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and commanded obedience to and submission to; yet they loathed them and were too arrogant to follow them.

The verse provides the reason for the content of the preceding verse, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ دَمَّرَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ أَمْثالُها "Have they not travelled through the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? Allah destroyed them, and for the faithless there will be the like of it" (47:10). Destruction (التدمير) means annihilation. It is said: "Allah destroyed him" (دمره الله) meaning He annihilated him. And it is said: "Allah brought destruction upon him" (دمر الله عليه) meaning He annihilated everything pertaining to him, including his person, family, house, and property. Thus, "brought destruction upon him" (دمر عليه) is more emphatic than "destroyed him" (دمره), as has been noted. The pronoun in "the like of it" (أَمْثالُها) refers to the "fate" (العاقبة) or the "punishment" (العقوبة) implied by the preceding discourse.

What is meant by "the faithless" (الكافرين) are those who disbelieved in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning is: and for those who disbelieve in you, O Muhammad, there shall be the likes of that fate or punishment. They were threatened with the "likes" (أمثال) of the fate or punishment — even though only one such fate will actually befall them — because they are exposed to many worldly and otherworldly punishments, even if only some of them actually occur. It is also possible that "the faithless" refers to disbelievers in general, making the sentence a statement of a universal principle.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector" (47:11). The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to what was previously mentioned regarding the helping of the believers, the detestation of the disbelievers, and their evil fate. No heed should be paid to the view that it refers to the confirmation of the fate or punishment of past nations for these people, nor to the view that it refers only to the helping of the believers; this is because the verse addresses the state of both groups: the believers and the disbelievers together.

The term "protector" (المولى) is as if it were a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) used in an adjectival sense, so it carries the meaning of "guardian" (الولي). For this reason, it is applied to the master and owner of a slave because he possesses the authority of disposal (ولاية التصرف) over the affairs of his slave. It is also applied to the helper (الناصر) because he takes charge of the affairs of the one he helps through strengthening and support. Allah, the Glorified, is the "Protector" (المولى) because He is the Owner who takes charge of the affairs of His creation in the path of the ontological creation (التكوين) and manages them as He wills. He, the Exalted, said: ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ "You have no guardian or intercessor besides Him" (32:4), and He said: وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ "and they will be returned to Allah, their true master" (10:30). He, the Exalted, is also the "Protector" (المولى) because He takes charge of managing the affairs of His servants in the path of felicity (السعادة), guiding them toward their bliss and Paradise, granting them success in righteous deeds (الصالحات), and helping them against their enemies. This second sense of "protection" (المولوية) is exclusive to the believers, for they are the ones who have entered the fold of servitude and follow what their Lord desires of them, unlike the disbelievers.

The believers have a Protector and Guardian (الولي), who is Allah, the Glorified, as He said: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful," and He said: اللهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Allah is the guardian of the faithful" (2:257). As for the disbelievers, they have taken idols or the lords of the idols as guardians; thus, they are their guardians according to their own claim. As such, He said, based on their false claim and by way of sarcasm: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ "As for the faithless, their patrons are the Rebels" (2:257). However, He negated their guardianship based on the reality of the matter, saying: وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ "and because the faithless have no protector." Then He negated their guardianship absolutely, both ontologically and legislatively, saying: أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ "Have they taken guardians besides Him? It is Allah who is the Guardian" (42:9), and He said: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ "These are but names which you have coined—you and your fathers" (53:23).

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: His helping the believers, His making their feet steady, His forsaking the disbelievers, His letting their deeds go astray, and His punishing them is all because He, the Exalted, is the Protector and Guardian of the believers, and because the disbelievers have no protector to help them, guide their deeds, or save them from His punishment.

From what has preceded, the weakness of the view that "Protector" (المولى) in this verse means "Helper" (الناصر) to the exclusion of "Owner" (المالك) becomes clear; otherwise, it would contradict His saying: وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ "and they will be returned to Allah, their true master" (10:30). The reason for this weakness is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ وَالنَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ "Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. As for the faithless, they enjoy themselves and eat like the cattle eat, and the Fire will be their abode" (47:12). This is a comparison between the two groups and an exposition of the effect of Allah’s guardianship (ولاية) over the believers and the lack of it for the disbelievers in terms of the final outcome and the Hereafter: that the believers enter Paradise while the disbelievers reside in the Fire.

The discourse points to the origin of this mentioned effect by describing each of the two groups in a manner suited to their ultimate state. He points to the attribute of the believers by His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ "those who have faith and do righteous deeds," and to the attribute of the disbelievers by His saying: يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ "they enjoy themselves and eat like the cattle eat." These two descriptions, through their mutual contrast, convey that the believers are rightly guided in their worldly life and have attained the truth, as they believed in Allah and performed righteous deeds, thereby following the path of rectitude and fulfilling the duty of humanity. As for the disbelievers, they have no concern for attaining the truth, nor are their hearts attached to the duties of humanity; rather, their sole concern is their bellies and their private parts. They enjoy themselves in their brief worldly life and eat as cattle eat, having no aspiration other than that and no goal beyond it.

These — the believers — are under the guardianship of Allah, as they follow a path their Lord desires for them and to which He guides them; therefore, He admits them in the Hereafter into gardens with streams running in them. Those — the disbelievers — have no guardian and have been left to themselves; therefore, their abode and residence is the Fire.

The admission of the believers into the gardens is attributed to Allah Himself, whereas the residence of the disbelievers in the Fire is not, in fulfillment of the right of the aforementioned guardianship. For He, the Exalted, has a special concern for His friends (أولياء); as for those who have stripped themselves of His guardianship, He does not care in which valley they perish.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ هِيَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِنْ قَرْيَتِكَ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ فَلا ناصِرَ لَهُمْ "How many a town was more powerful than your town which expelled you, and We destroyed them, and they had no helper" (47:13). What is meant by "town" (القرية) is the people of the town, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: أَهْلَكْناهُمْ "We destroyed them," etc. The town that expelled him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is Mecca.

In this verse is a strengthening of the heart of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a threat to the people of Mecca, and a belittling of their power: that Allah destroyed many towns, each of which was more powerful than their town, and they had no helper to assist them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ "Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their deeds has been made to seem fair and who follow their low desires?" (47:14). The context, which proceeds by comparing the state of the believers with that of the disbelievers, indicates that those who "stand on a manifest proof from their Lord" are the believers. What is meant by their being on a manifest proof (بينة) — a clear evidence that necessitates certainty — from their Lord is that they possess a clear indication from their Lord that necessitates certainty regarding what they believe, which is the demonstrative proof (الحجة البرهانية). Thus, they only follow conclusive proof, as is befitting for a human being whose nature is to employ reason and follow the truth.

As for those who disbelieve, they have been captivated by their evil deeds which Satan made to seem fair to them, and to which their low desires (أهواء) became attached, so they committed sins. How great is the difference between the two groups!

His saying, the Exalted: مَثْلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ "A description of the paradise promised to the God-conscious..." (47:15) until the end of the verse. It distinguishes between the two groups by explaining the outcome of their affairs. In reality, it is an elucidation of what was previously mentioned in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith..." etc., regarding the difference between them. Thus, this verse is effectively a detailed exposition of that verse.

His saying: مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ "A description of the paradise promised to the God-conscious." The word "description" (المثل) here means "attribute" (الصفة) — as has been said — meaning the attribute of the Paradise that Allah promised to admit the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness and protect themselves from Allah's displeasure — into. Perhaps "description" (المثل) is taken in its well-known sense of "parable," from which it is understood that Paradise is too sublime and exalted for any description to encompass or for any words to define; rather, it is brought closer to the minds through parables, as alluded to in His saying: فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ "No one knows what has been kept hidden for them of the delight of the eyes" (32:17).

In this verse, His saying in the previous verse: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ "those who have faith and do righteous deeds" has been replaced by His saying: الْمُتَّقُونَ "the God-conscious." This is a replacement of the consequent by the antecedent, for godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah necessitates faith in Him and the performance of righteous deeds.

His saying: فِيها أَنْهارٌ مِنْ ماءٍ غَيْرِ آسِنٍ "in it are rivers of water unstaling" (47:15), meaning it does not change by remaining for a long time. His saying: وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ لَبَنٍ لَمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُ "and rivers of milk whose taste does not change," unlike the milks of this world. His saying: وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ خَمْرٍ لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ "and rivers of wine delicious to the drinkers." The word "delicious" (لذة) is either a feminine adjectival noun describing the wine, or an infinitive used as an adjective for emphasis, or it implies a suppressed possessive, meaning "possessing deliciousness." His saying: وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ عَسَلٍ مُصَفًّى "and rivers of honey clarified," meaning pure from wax, froth, impurities, and all other annoyances and defects found in the honey of this world. His saying: وَلَهُمْ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ "and in it they will have every kind of fruit," using the plural for universality.

His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ "and forgiveness from their Lord," by which every sin and evil deed is erased from them, so their life is not clouded by any distress nor spoiled by any annoyance. In the expression "from their Lord" (مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ) is an allusion to the enveloping of mercy and the encompassing of divine affection and kindness.

His saying: كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ فِي النَّارِ "Are they like those who are immortal in the Fire?" This is a comparison with one side suppressed, meaning: Is he who enters the Paradise of which this is the description like him who is immortal in the Fire and whose drink is water of intense heat that cuts their bowels and the internal organs in their bellies when they are made to drink it? They are only made to drink it while being coerced, as in His saying: وَسُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ "and are given to drink boiling water that cuts up their bowels" (47:15). It has been said that His saying: كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ "like those who are immortal..." etc., is an elucidation of His saying in the previous verse: كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ "like those to whom [the evil of their deeds] has been made to seem fair..." etc., but this is as you see [unlikely].

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ "That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down," Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "They loathed what Allah sent down regarding the right of Ali (peace be upon him)."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ "like those to whom the evil of their deeds has been made to seem fair," it is said: "They are the hypocrites." This is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

I say: It is possible that both narrations are instances of "conceptual application" (الجري) — the application of a verse to a specific instance that fits its general meaning.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ فِي النَّارِ وَسُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ "Are they like those who are immortal in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water that cuts up their bowels?" he said: "He who is in this described Paradise is not like him who is in this Fire, just as the enemy of Allah is not like His friend."

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا خَرَجُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مَاذَا قَالَ ءَانِفًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ (١٦)
There are some among them who prick up their ears at you. But when they go out from your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’ They are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal, and they follow their own desires. (16)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ زَادَهُمْ هُدًى وَءَاتَىٰهُمْ تَقْوَىٰهُمْ (١٧)
As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances their guidance and invests them with their Godwariness. (17)
فَهَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَ أَشْرَاطُهَا ۚ فَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمْ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ذِكْرَىٰهُمْ (١٨)
Do they await anything except that the Hour should overtake them suddenly? Its portents have already come. When it overtakes them of what avail will the admonitions they were given? (18)
فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْوَىٰكُمْ (١٩)
Know that there is no god except Allah, and plead [to Allah] for forgiveness of your sin and for the faithful, men and women. Allah knows your itinerary and your [final] abode. (19)
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ ۖ فَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُّحْكَمَةٌ وَذُكِرَ فِيهَا ٱلْقِتَالُ ۙ رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ ٱلْمَغْشِىِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٠)
The faithful say, ‘If only a surah were sent down!’ But when a conclusive surah is sent down and war is mentioned in it, you see those in whose hearts is a sickness looking upon you with the look of someone fainting at death. So woe to them! (20)
طَاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ ۚ فَإِذَا عَزَمَ ٱلْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ (٢١)
Obedience and upright speech.... So when the matter has been resolved upon [concerning going to war], if they remain true to Allah that will surely be better for them. (21)
فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوٓا۟ أَرْحَامَكُمْ (٢٢)
May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and ill-treat your blood relations? (22)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٣)
They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their sight. (23)
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَآ (٢٤)
Do they not contemplate the Quran, or are there locks on the hearts? (24)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَى ۙ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٥)
Indeed those who turned their backs after the guidance had become clear to them, it was Satan who had seduced them, and he had given them [far-flung] hopes. (25)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا۟ مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِى بَعْضِ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرَارَهُمْ (٢٦)
That is because they said to those who loathed what Allah had sent down: ‘We shall obey you in some matters,’ and Allah knows their secret dealings. (26)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٧)
But how will it be [with them] when the angels take them away, striking their faces and their backs?! (27)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَآ أَسْخَطَ ٱللَّهَ وَكَرِهُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٢٨)
That, because they pursued what displeased Allah, and loathed His pleasure. So He has made their works fail. (28)
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَن لَّن يُخْرِجَ ٱللَّهُ أَضْغَـٰنَهُمْ (٢٩)
Do those in whose hearts is a sickness suppose that Allah will not expose their spite? (29)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَأَرَيْنَـٰكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ ۚ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِى لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ (٣٠)
If We wish, We will show them to you so that you recognize them by their mark. Yet you will recognize them by their tone of speech, and Allah knows your deeds. (30)
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا۟ أَخْبَارَكُمْ (٣١)
We will surely test you until We ascertain those of you who wage jihad and those who are steadfast, and We shall appraise your record. (31)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَشَآقُّوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٣٢)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become clear to them, they will not hurt Allah in the least, and He shall make their works fail. (32)

(Commentary)

The verses continue according to the previous context, and within them is an address regarding the state of those in whose hearts is a sickness, the hypocrites, and those who turned back after their belief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّى إِذا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ ما ذا قالَ آنِفاً — "Among them are those who listen to you, but when they leave your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’" (47:16) etc. The word ānifan (آنفاً) is an active participle (اسم فاعل) in the accusative case as an adverb of time (ظرفية) or as an object of time (مفعول فيه). Its meaning is the hour just before your current hour. It is also said to mean "this very hour." In any case, it is derived from al-anf (الأنف), meaning the organ [the nose], [implying the very tip or beginning of something].

His saying: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ — "Among them are those who listen to you." The pronoun refers to those who disbelieve. What is meant by their listening to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is their lending an ear to what he recites of the Quran and what he explains to them of the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) and the laws of the religion (شرائع الدين).

His saying: حَتَّى إِذا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ — "but when they leave your presence." The pronoun refers to the relative pronoun [those who], and the pronoun is pluralized in consideration of the meaning, just as it was singular in يَسْتَمِعُ ("listens") in consideration of the literal word.

His saying: قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ ما ذا قالَ آنِفاً — "they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’" What is meant by "those who have been given knowledge" are the scholars of Allah among the Companions. The pronoun in "What did he say" refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The inquiry in their saying, "What did he say just now?" is said to be for actual information, because their immersion in arrogance, pride, and the following of whims (اتباع الأهواء) would not let them understand the truthful word, as the Exalted said: فَما لِهؤُلاءِ الْقَوْمِ لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثاً — "What is the matter with this people that they do not almost understand any discourse?" (4:78). It is also said to be for mockery, and it is said to be for belittlement—as if the speech, being filled with falsehoods [in their view], does not yield any coherent meaning. There is a justification for each of these three meanings.

His saying: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ — "They are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal" (47:16) is a description of them. His saying: وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ — "and they follow their [own] desires" (47:16) is a further description after the first, functioning like an explanatory apposition (عطف التفسير). What results from this is that the following of whims is a sign of the sealing of the heart (الطبع على القلب); for the heart that is not sealed, which remains upon the purity of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — does not hesitate in understanding religious knowledge and divine realities.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً وَآتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ — "As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances them in guidance and invests them with their God-consciousness" (47:17). The apparent contrast between this verse and the preceding one indicates that what is meant by guidance (الاهتداء) is that which opposes the misguidance (الضلال) necessitated by the sealing of the heart; it is the submission to that which the sound primordial nature (الفطرة) guides toward and the following of the truth. The "enhancement of their guidance" from Allah, the Glorified, is His raising the degree of their faith (الإيمان). It has been previously mentioned that guidance (الهدى) and faith possess various levels. What is meant by God-consciousness (التقوى) — the state of godwariness and protection against spiritual harm — is that which opposes the following of whims; it is the wariness (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah and the avoidance of committing sins.

Through this, it becomes clear that the enhancement of guidance refers to their perfection in the realm of knowledge (العلم), and the granting of God-consciousness refers to their perfection in the realm of action (العمل). It also appears through the contrast that the sealing of the hearts refers to their lack of perfection in knowledge, while the following of whims refers to their lack of righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) and their deprivation thereof. This does not contradict what we previously stated: that the following of whims is like an explanatory apposition to the sealing of the hearts.

His saying, the Exalted: فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً فَقَدْ جاءَ أَشْراطُها — "Do they await anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly? Its tokens have already come" (47:18) etc. "Looking" (النظر) here means waiting (الانتظار). "Tokens" (الأشراط) is the plural of sharaṭ (شرط), meaning a sign (العلامة). The linguistic origin of its meaning is "condition" (الشرط) — that upon which the existence of a thing depends — because the realization of the condition is a sign of the realization of the thing itself. Thus, the tokens of the Hour (أشراط الساعة) are its signs that point toward it.

The context of the verse is one of sarcasm (التهكم), as if they are standing in a position where they must either follow the truth, whereby their end would be felicitous, or wait for the Hour until, when they become certain of its occurrence and are on the verge of it, they remember, believe, and follow the truth. As for following the truth today, they have not submitted to it through any proof, exhortation, or lesson. As for their waiting for the coming of the Hour so that they may remember then, it will not benefit them at all, for it comes suddenly (بغتة) and grants them no respite to prepare for it through remembrance. When it occurs, remembrance will not benefit them because that day is a day of recompense (جزاء), not a day of action (عمل). The Exalted said: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ وَأَنَّى لَهُ الذِّكْرى يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي قَدَّمْتُ لِحَياتِي — "on that day man will take admonition, but what will the admonition avail him? He will say, ‘Alas, I wish I had sent forth [some good deeds] for my life!’" (89:23-24).

Furthermore, its tokens and signs have already come and been realized. Perhaps what is meant by its tokens is the creation of man and the division of his species into the righteous and the corrupt, the God-conscious and the profligate, which necessitates a decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) between them and the descent of death upon them; for all of that is among the conditions for the occurrence of the Event and the coming of the Hour. It is also said that the tokens of the Hour refer to the appearance of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who is the Seal of the Prophets, the splitting of the moon, and the revelation of the Quran, which is the last of the Heavenly Books.

This is the meaning yielded by contemplation (التدبر) upon the verse, and it is—as you see—a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) even as it is presented in a sarcastic manner.

Accordingly, His saying: بَغْتَةً ("suddenly") is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) of the "coming," mentioned to describe the reality and to serve as the basis for His subsequent saying: فَأَنَّى لَهُمْ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ ذِكْراهُمْ ("then how will their admonition avail them when it has [already] come to them?"). It is not a restriction on the "waiting" such that it would imply they are only waiting for it to come suddenly. To repel this misconception, it was said: إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً ("anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly") and it did not say: "except that the Hour should come upon them suddenly."

His saying: فَأَنَّى لَهُمْ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ ذِكْراهُمْ — "then how will their admonition avail them when it has [already] come to them?" (47:18). Annā (أنى) is the advanced predicate (خبر مقدم) and dhikrāhum (ذِكْراهُمْ) is the delayed subject (مبتدأ مؤخر), while "when it has come to them" is a parenthetical clause between them. The meaning is: How will it be possible for them to remember when it has come? That is, how will they benefit from remembrance on a day when the action performed therein does not benefit, as it is only the day of recompense.

The commentators have various opinions regarding the meaning of the sentences of the verse and its overall meaning, which we have omitted; whoever desires them should refer to their detailed books.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ — "Know then that there is no god but Allah, and ask forgiveness for your sin and for the faithful men and faithful women" (47:19) etc. It is said that this is branched (متفرع) from all that preceded in the Surah regarding the felicity of the believers and the wretchedness of the disbelievers.

It is as if it were said: Since you know that the matter is as mentioned regarding the felicity of these and the wretchedness of those, then remain firm upon what you possess of the knowledge of the oneness of Allah (التوحيد), the Glorified. Thus, the meaning of the command to "know" (العلم) according to this is the command to remain firm (الثبات) upon knowledge.

It is also possible that it is branched from what He explained in the two preceding verses—namely His saying: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ ("Among them are those who listen to you") until His saying: وَآتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ ("and invests them with their God-consciousness")—regarding how He seals the hearts of the polytheists and leaves them to their sins, while reversing the matter for those who are guided to His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and belief in Him. It is as if it were said: Since the matter is so, then hold fast to your knowledge of the oneness of the Deity and seek forgiveness for your sin and for the believers of your community, both men and women, so that you may not be among those whose hearts Allah seals and whom He deprives of God-consciousness by leaving them to their sins. This view is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْواكُمْ ("And Allah knows your moving about and your abiding").

Thus, His saying: فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ means, according to what the context supports: "Hold fast to your knowledge that there is no god but Allah." His saying: وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ ("and ask forgiveness for your sin")—the discussion regarding the meaning of "sin" (الذنب) attributed to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has preceded, and it will also come in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Fath, if Allah wills.

His saying: وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ ("and for the faithful men and faithful women") is a command to seek forgiveness for the community of believers, both men and women. It is far-removed from the Exalted that He should command the seeking of forgiveness and not meet it with forgiveness, or command a supplication and not meet it with an answer.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْواكُمْ — "And Allah knows your moving about and your abiding" (47:19) is a justification for what is in the beginning of the verse: "Know then that..." etc. It is apparent that mutaqallab (المتقلب) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning the transition from one state to another, and likewise mathwā (المثوى) means stability and rest. The meaning is that He, the Exalted, knows all your states, whether changing or constant, motion or rest; so remain firm upon His monotheism, seek His forgiveness, and beware lest He seal your hearts and leave you to your whims.

It is also said that "moving about" and "abiding" refer to conduct in the life of this world and the residence in the Hereafter. It is said that "moving about" is the transition from the loins to the wombs, and "abiding" is the resting in the earth.

It is said that "moving about" is conduct while awake and "abiding" is sleep. It is said that "moving about" is conduct in livelihoods and earnings, and "abiding" is staying in homes. What we have previously stated is more apparent and more general.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ — "The faithful say, ‘Why has not a surah been sent down?’" (47:20) until the end of the verse. Lawlā (لو لا) is for instigation (تحضيضية), meaning "Why was not a Surah sent down?" whereby they manifest a desire for the revelation of a new Surah that would bring them new obligations (تكاليف) which they would fulfill. What is meant by "Surah" is a definitive (محكمة) and clear one in which there is no ambiguity (تشابه). What is meant by the "mention of fighting" is the command for it.

What is meant by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are those weak in faith among the believers, not the hypocrites; for the verse is explicit that those who manifested the desire for its revelation were "those who believe." "Those who believe" does not encompass the hypocrites except by way of a laxity (مساهلة) that is inappropriate for the Speech of Allah. Thus, the verse is like His saying, the Exalted, regarding a group of the believers: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتالُ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ النَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ اللهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً — "Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from fighting], and maintain the prayer and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them, behold, a part of them were afraid of people as if they were afraid of Allah, or were even more afraid" (4:77).

The one "fainting at [the approach of] death" (المغشي عليه من الموت) is the one in the throes of death (المحتضر). It is said ghashiyahu ghashāwah (غشيه غشاوة) when something veils or covers him. Ghashiya ‘alā fulān (غشي على فلان)—in the passive—is said when something overcomes his intellect. The "look of one fainting at death" is his staring at you with his eyes fixed without blinking.

His saying: فَأَوْلى لَهُمْ — "So woe to them!" (47:20). Perhaps it is a predicate for a deleted subject, the estimation being: "That is more appropriate for them," meaning it is fitting for them to look in such a way—that is, to be in the throes of death and die. It is narrated from al-Asma‘i that their saying awlā laka (أَوْلى لَكَ) is a word of threat meaning "May that which you dislike befall you and accompany you." The verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: أَوْلى لَكَ فَأَوْلى ثُمَّ أَوْلى لَكَ فَأَوْلى — "Woe to you! And then woe to you! Again, woe to you! And then woe to you!" (75:34-35).

The meaning of the verse is: The believers say, "Why has not a Surah been sent down?" but when a definitive Surah is sent down in which there is no ambiguity, and they are commanded therein to fight and perform jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah — you see those weak in faith among them looking at you, out of intense fear, with the look of one in the throes of death; so that is more appropriate for them.

His saying, the Exalted: طاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ فَإِذا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللهَ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ — "Obedience and a worthy speech. Then, when the matter is resolved, if they are true to Allah, it will surely be better for them" (47:21). "The matter is resolved" (عزم الأمر) means it has become serious and finalized.

His saying: طاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ — "Obedience and a worthy speech" is as if it were a predicate for a deleted subject, the estimation being: "Our command—or their command and state—is obedience." That is, their faith in Us is an obedience they pledged to Us, and a worthy speech (قول معروف), not a reprehensible one, which they said to Us, which is the manifestation of hearing and obeying, as the Exalted recounts from them in His saying: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well]..." until He said: وَقالُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا — "...and they say, ‘We hear and obey’" (2:285).

Based on this, His subsequent saying: فَإِذا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللهَ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ ("Then, when the matter is resolved, if they are true to Allah, it will surely be better for them") connects to what preceded it with a clear connection. The meaning is: The "matter" is that which they pledged to Allah in their saying, "We hear and obey." So if, when the matter was resolved, they had been true to Allah in what they said and obeyed Him in what He commanded—including the command to fight—it would have been better for them.

It is also possible that His saying: طاعَةٌ ("Obedience") etc., is a predicate for a pronoun referring back to the aforementioned "fighting." The estimation would be: The fighting mentioned in the Surah is an "obedience" from them and a "worthy speech." So if, when the matter was resolved, they had been true to Allah in their faith and obeyed Him therein, it would have been better for them. As for it being an "obedience" from them, that is clear. As for it being a "worthy speech," it is because the obligation of fighting and the command to defend the righteous society to nullify the plots of its enemies is a worthy speech recognized by reason and the rational (العقلاء).

It is said that His saying: طاعَةٌ ("Obedience") etc., is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate is deleted, the estimation being: "Obedience and a worthy speech are better for them and more exemplary." It is also said it is a subject whose predicate is فَأَوْلى لَهُمْ ("So woe to them!") in the previous verse, making the verse a completion of the previous one; this is a weak opinion. Even weaker is the opinion that "Obedience" etc., is an adjective for "Surah" in His saying: "But when a [definitive] surah is sent down." Other things have also been said.

His saying, the Exalted: فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَنْ تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحامَكُمْ — "May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and ill-treat your blood relations?" (47:22). The address is to those in whose hearts is a sickness, who are sluggish regarding the matter of jihad in the way of Allah. The address has turned toward them (التفات) to increase the rebuke and reprimand. The inquiry is for affirmation (تقرير). "Turning away" (التولي) means averting oneself, and what is meant is averting oneself from the Book of Allah and acting upon what is in it, and returning to polytheism and rejecting the religion.

The meaning is: Is it to be expected of you, if you turn away from the Book of Allah and acting upon what is in it—including jihad in the way of Allah—that you would cause corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship (تقطعوا أرحامكم) by shedding blood, plundering wealth, and violating honors, out of greed for the carcass of this world? That is, if you turn away, this is what would be expected of you.

Through this, it has become clear that the verse is in the position of justifying His saying in the previous verse: "it will surely be better for them," which is why it began with the particle fā’ (ف).

It is said that "turning away" (التولي) means assuming governance and authority (الولاية). The meaning would be: Is it to be expected of you, if you are made governors, that you would cause corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship by shedding forbidden blood, taking bribes, and being unjust in judgment? This meaning is far-removed from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمى أَبْصارَهُمْ — "They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their eyes" (47:23). The indication is toward those who cause corruption in the land and sever ties of kinship. Allah has described them as being cursed by Him, so He made them deaf and took away their hearing such that they do not hear the truthful word, and He blinded their eyes such that they do not see the truthful view; for it is not the eyes that go blind, but the hearts within the breasts that go blind.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ أَمْ عَلى قُلُوبٍ أَقْفالُها — "Do they not contemplate the Quran, or are there locks on the hearts?" (47:24). The inquiry is for rebuke, and the plural pronoun refers to those mentioned in the previous verse. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "hearts" (قُلُوبٍ) is, as has been said, to indicate that what is meant are the hearts of these people and their likes.

It is said in al-Majma‘ al-Bayan: "In this is a proof for the invalidity of the view of those who say: It is not permissible to interpret anything from the outward meaning (ظاهر) of the Quran except through a report (خبر) and audition (سمع)." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ ارْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَى الشَّيْطانُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلى لَهُمْ — "Indeed those who turned their backs after guidance had become manifest to them, it was Satan who seduced them and gave them false hopes" (47:25). "Turning back on their heels" (الارتداد على الأدبار) is returning to the rear after facing forward; it is a metaphor intended to mean abandonment after acceptance. "Seduction" (التسويل) is the beautifying of that which the soul incites toward, and the depicting of the ugly to it in the form of the beautiful. What is meant by "giving false hopes" (الإملاء) is providing extension or lengthening expectations.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرارَهُمْ — "That is because they said to those who detest what Allah has sent down, ‘We will obey you in some matters,’ and Allah knows their secrets" (47:26). The indication "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to Satan's seduction and his giving them false hopes—in short, his mastery over them. What is meant by "those who detest what Allah has sent down" are those who disbelieve, as previously mentioned in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ — "As for the faithless, let them be miserable, and He has made their deeds go awry. That is because they detested what Allah has sent down..." (47:8-9).

His saying: سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ — "We will obey you in some matters" is the content of their speech and a promise from them to the disbelievers of obedience. As suggested by the restriction of obedience to "some matters" in a vague manner, it is the speech of one who is unable to openly manifest obedience to the one he wishes to obey in all matters, because he is in danger from manifesting absolute obedience. Thus, he secretly tells the one he promises that he will obey him in some matters and in whatever is feasible for him, then he conceals that and sits waiting for a turn of fortune.

It is inferred from this that these were a group of hypocrites who secretly conveyed to the disbelievers what the Exalted recounted from them, promising them obedience whenever it was feasible for them. This view is supported by His saying afterward: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرارَهُمْ ("and Allah knows their secrets").

They have differed as to who these people were. It is said they were the Jews who said to the hypocrites: "If you announce disbelief, we will help you." It is said they were the Jews, or the Jews and the hypocrites, who said that to the polytheists. Both views are refuted by the fact that the subject of the verse is those who "turned back" (ارتدوا) after their belief, and the Jews did not believe such that they could turn back.

It is said they were the hypocrites who promised the Jews help, as the Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَإِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ — "Have you not regarded the hypocrites who say to their brethren who are faithless among the People of the Book, ‘If you are expelled, we will surely go out with you, and we will never obey anyone against you; and if you are fought, we will surely help you’?" (59:11).

In this regard, the verse is capable of being applied to that, just as it is capable of being applied to the Jews in their promise of help to the polytheists—with some strain in applying "turning back" (الارتداد) to their disbelief in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after his messengership had become manifest to them. However, there is no evidence from the literal wording of the verse for that; perhaps they were a group of hypocrites other than them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَيْفَ إِذا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبارَهُمْ — "But how will it be when the angels take them away, striking their faces and their backs?" (47:27) is branched from what preceded it. The meaning is: This is their state today, turning back after guidance has become manifest to them and doing as they wish; so how will their state be when the angels take them in death while striking their faces and their backs?

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اتَّبَعُوا ما أَسْخَطَ اللهَ وَكَرِهُوا رِضْوانَهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "That is because they pursued what displeases Allah and detested His pleasure, so He has made their deeds go awry" (47:28). It is apparent that what is meant by "what displeases Allah" are the whims of the soul and the seductions of Satan that lead to sins and ruinous offenses, as the Exalted said: "and they follow their [own] desires," and He said: "it was Satan who seduced them and gave them false hopes."

Displeasure (السخط) and pleasure (الرضا) are among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), and what is meant by them is punishment and reward.

The indication in His saying: ذلِكَ ("That") refers to what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the angels' punishment of them at the time of their death. That is, the cause of their punishment is that their deeds are nullified (حابطة) because of their following what displeases Allah and their detesting His pleasure; and since they have no righteous deeds, they are wretched with punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ أَنْ لَنْ يُخْرِجَ اللهُ أَضْغانَهُمْ — "Do those in whose hearts is a sickness suppose that Allah will not expose their malice?" (47:29). Al-Raghib said: Al-ḍighn (الضغن)—with a kasrah on the ḍād—and al-ḍughn (الضغن)—with a ḍammah—is intense malice (الحقد الشديد), and its plural is aḍghān (أضغان). End quote. What is meant by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are those weak in faith. Perhaps they are those who believed initially with a weakness in their faith, then inclined toward hypocrisy and turned back after belief. Careful contemplation of the history of early Islam clarifies that a group of those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were of this description, just as another group of them were hypocrites from the first day they believed until the end of their lives. Accordingly, their being counted among the believers in what preceded is in consideration of the beginning of their affair.

The meaning is: Rather, did these hypocrites in whose hearts is a sickness think that Allah would not bring out and manifest their malice toward the religion and its people?

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَأَرَيْناكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمالَكُمْ — "Had We wished, We would have showed them to you and you would have recognized them by their mark; and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech, and Allah knows your deeds" (47:30). Sīmā (السيماء) is the mark (العلامة). The meaning is: Had We wished, We would have shown you those with diseased hearts, and you would have recognized them by their mark which We would have made known to you.

His saying: وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ — "and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech." Al-Raghib said: Al-laḥn (اللحن) is turning speech away from its established course: either by removing the inflection (إعراب) or mispronunciation (تصحيف), which is blameworthy and more commonly used; or by removing it from explicitness and turning it toward allusion (تعريض) and implication (فحوى), which is praised by most men of letters in terms of eloquence. End quote.

The meaning is: You will surely recognize them from the nature of their speech by what it contains of metaphor and allusion. In making the "tone of speech" (لحن القول) the vessel for recognition, there is a type of metaphorical emphasis.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمالَكُمْ — "and Allah knows your deeds" (47:30). That is, He knows their realities and from which intentions and purposes they issued, so He will recompense the believers for their righteous deeds and others for theirs. In this is a promise for the believers and a threat for others.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّى نَعْلَمَ الْمُجاهِدِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّابِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا أَخْبارَكُمْ — "We will surely test you until We ascertain those of you who wage jihad and those who are steadfast, and We will assess your reports" (47:31). Al-balā’ (البلاء) and al-ibtilā’ (الابتلاء) are trial and testing. The verse is an explanation of the reason for prescribing fighting for the believers: it is the divine test (الاختبار الإلهي) to distinguish the mujahideen (المجاهدين) — those who strive in the way of Allah — and those who are patient (الصابرين) — those who remain steadfast through the hardships of divine obligations.

His saying: وَنَبْلُوَا أَخْبارَكُمْ — "and We will assess your reports." It is as if what is meant by "reports" (الأخبار) are the deeds, insofar as they issue from the doers and thus become reports about them. Through the testing of deeds, the righteous are distinguished from the corrupt, just as through the testing of souls, the righteous and good souls are distinguished. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by the "knowledge" (العلم) resulting for the Exalted from testing His servants is the manifestation of the servants' state through that; and from a more precise perspective, it is an active knowledge (علم فعلي) of the Exalted that is external to the Essence.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدى لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللهَ شَيْئاً وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become manifest to them, they will not hurt Allah in the least, and He will make their deeds go awry" (47:32). What is meant by these are the leaders of misguidance among the disbelievers of Mecca and those who join them, because they are the ones who barred from the way of Allah and defied the Messenger and opposed him with the most intense enmity after guidance had become manifest to them.

His saying: لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللهَ شَيْئاً — "they will not hurt Allah in the least" because the plot and ruse of man only return upon himself and do not harm anyone but him. His saying: وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ — "and He will make their deeds go awry" means their efforts to demolish the foundation of the religion and what they did to extinguish the light of Allah. It is also said that what is meant is the nullification of their deeds such that they are not rewarded in the Hereafter for any of their actions. The first meaning is more suitable to the context because it contains an incitement of the believers and an encouragement for them to fight the polytheists, and a comforting of their souls that they are the victors, as the following verses indicate.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ ("Among them are those who listen to you") etc., narrated from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from Ali (عليه‌السلام), who said: "We were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and he would inform us of the revelation—so I would comprehend it, as would whoever else comprehended it—but when we left, they would say: 'What did he say just now?'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Tirmidhi narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I and the Hour have been sent like these two,' and he pointed with his index and middle fingers."

I say: This wording has been narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through other chains from Abu Hurayrah and Sahl ibn Mas‘ud.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Majah, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "One day the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was appearing before the people, and a man came to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, when is the Hour?'
